message.content:
❯ run `find .` after sshing to `bhyslop-nas2`; you will see that I have several copies of very very large files.  Help me
  come up with a plan to deduplicate and validate each.  They are password protected and it is a bother retrieving the
  right password.  Calculating the md5sum takes surprisingly long- seems wrong since copying large file is so much faster.  thoughts?

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Deduplicate large password-protected files on NAS

content.text: I'll SSH in and take a look at what's there.

input.command: ssh bhyslop-nas2 'pwd; echo "---"; uname -a; echo "---"; nproc; echo "---"; cat /proc/cpuinfo | grep -m1 "model name" || true'
input.description: SSH in, check host and CPU

content.text: Lots of duplication across backup roots. Let me pull the full inventory and group it.

input.command: ssh bhyslop-nas2 'find . -type f -printf "%s\t%f\t%p\n" 2>/dev/null' > /tmp/nas_inventory.tsv; wc -l /tmp/nas_inventory.tsv; echo "=== total bytes ==="; awk -F'\t' '{s+=$1} END{printf "%.2f TB across %d files\n", s/1099511627776, NR}' /tmp/nas_inventory.tsv; echo "=== distinct (size,basename) groups and how many copies ==="; awk -F'\t' '{print $1"\t"$2}' /tmp/nas_inventory.tsv | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -40
input.description: Fetch full inventory, summarize duplication

lastPrompt: ❯ run `find .` after sshing to `bhyslop-nas2`; you will see that I have several copies of very very large files.  Help me   come up with a plan to deduplicate and validate each.  They are password pro…

aiTitle: Deduplicate large password-protected files on NAS

content.text: Now I understand the shape of this. Before I write up the plan, let me check what tools and filesystem you have to work with.

input.command: ssh bhyslop-nas2 'echo "=== fs type & mounts ==="; df -hT /volume1 2>/dev/null || df -h .; echo "=== tools ==="; for t in md5sum sha256sum xxhsum xxh128sum b3sum 7z 7za 7zz rdfind jdupes hardlink openssl; do printf "%-10s " "$t"; command -v "$t" || echo "(absent)"; done'
input.description: Check filesystem type and available hashing/dedup tools

content.content:
=== fs type & mounts ===
Filesystem        Type   Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/vg1/volume_1 btrfs  8.8T  8.6T  208G  98% /volume1
=== tools ===
md5sum     /usr/bin/md5sum
sha256sum  /usr/bin/sha256sum
xxhsum     (absent)
xxh128sum  (absent)
b3sum      (absent)
7z         /usr/bin/7z
7za        (absent)

aiTitle: Deduplicate large password-protected files on NAS

content.text:
I have the full picture now. Here's what's going on — including the answer to your md5 puzzle.

## The puzzle, solved

Your NAS is a **Synology DS223j** — a quad-core ARM Cortex-A55 (`aarch64`, Realtek RTD1619b). That CPU has no crypto acceleration, so `md5sum` runs single-threaded at maybe ~150–250 MB/s. A 191 GB archive therefore takes ~15–20 min of pure CPU grinding, *reading every byte*.

Copying feels faster for a different reason: your volume is **btrfs**, and `find` reports **18.3 TB of files living on an 8.8 TB volume**. That's physically impossible unless extents are shared — i.e. Synology's `cp` on the same btrfs volume does **reflink (copy-on-write)**: it duplicates metadata only and moves *zero* bytes. Instant, regardless of size.

So: **copy = metadata-only reflink (no bytes read); md5 = every byte read through a slow ARM core.** They're not comparable operations. The fix for slow hashing is a faster algorithm (xxHash/BLAKE3) — but those aren't installed and a DS223j isn't easy to add them to. More importantly, **most of your hashing is unnecessary** (below).

## What's actually there

- **277 files, 18.3 TB logical** — but only **24 distinct archives = 1.65 TB of unique payload**.
- The rest are **2–17 redundant copies** each, scattered across consolidation roots (`bobbie/`, `fromdan/`, `FROM_SILVER--{1,2,3}`, `FROM_SEAGATE_3TB--*`, `FROM_BLUE--*`, `bhyslop-vault/`) — the residue of folding many old drives together.
- Collapsing to one copy each reclaims **~16.7 TB logical**. (Volume is **98% full, 208 GB free** — so this matters, and physical usage of 8.6 TB vs. 1.65 TB unique means *most copies are real physical duplicates, not yet shared* — deleting them frees real space.)

## The key insight for your plan

**Deduplication does not need the password.** Dedup only asks "are these two files byte-identical?" — that's a property of the *ciphertext*, answerable by hashing/comparing the encrypted bytes. The password is only needed to validate *archive contents*, which is a separate, once-per-distinct-archive job — not 274 jobs.

And you don't need to hash 18 TB to dedup safely. Within each `(size, basename)` group you can confirm identity with a **partial fingerprint** — size + first 16 MB + last 16 MB. For non-adversarial backup archives that's effectively certain, and it drops 18 TB of reading to a few GB (minutes, not days).

## Proposed plan (phased, resumable)

1. **Relieve pressure first** (you're at 98%): dedup is delete-heavy; doing it sooner reduces risk of a full-volume stall.
2. **Group by size** (already done, free) — 3 sizes are singletons and never need touching.
3. **Partial-fingerprint** every file in a multi-member group → confirm which copies are truly identical. Cheap.
4. **Collapse** each distinct archive to your chosen retention (see questions below).
5. **Validate the survivors only** — full read, once per retained copy: either `7z t` (needs password, proves internal integrity incl. bit-rot, run 24× max) or a full `md5sum`/`sha256sum` cross-check between retained copies (no password). This is where the unavoidable full-read cost lives, but it's ~1.65–3.3 TB, not 18 TB.

Two decisions drive the mechanics:

questions.question: How many independent copies of each distinct archive do you want to keep on bhyslop-nas2?

options.label: Keep 1 (Recommended)
options.description: One copy per archive. Reclaims the most space (~16.7 TB). The volume is RAID/SHR + you presumably have offsite backups, so on-NAS copies add little protection. Most aggressive.

options.label: Keep 2
options.description: Two copies of each, in different roots, as a hedge against accidental deletion or localized corruption. Reclaims somewhat less.

options.label: Decide per-archive
options.description: I report the duplicate map and you choose retention archive-by-archive before anything is deleted.

questions.question: How should redundant copies be removed?

options.label: Delete (Recommended)
options.description: rm the redundant copies. Frees real physical space — important at 98% full. Simple and reversible only via your backups.

options.label: Reflink-collapse
options.description: Replace dupes with cp --reflink so all paths survive but share extents. Preserves directory structure, frees space, but a single bad sector then damages all linked copies (no redundancy).

options.label: Hardlink-collapse
options.description: Replace dupes with hardlinks. Like reflink but all paths point to one inode; editing one would affect all. Saves space, keeps paths, zero redundancy.

questions.question: How thoroughly do you want the kept copies validated?

options.label: 7z integrity test (Recommended)
options.description: Run 7z t once per distinct archive (needs password, 24x max). Proves the archive is internally intact (catches bit-rot). You retrieve the password 24 times total, not 274.

options.label: Hash cross-check only
options.description: Full md5/sha256 of retained copies, no password. Proves copies match each other and detects divergence, but can't confirm the archive itself is internally valid.

options.label: Both
options.description: Hash cross-check for identity AND 7z t for internal integrity. Most thorough, most time + most password retrievals.

content.text:
Got it: **keep 2, delete the rest, validate by hash cross-check (no password).** That's a clean, password-free workflow.

Here's the safe execution order I'll follow — note that the actual `rm` of ~16 TB of your backups is the one irreversible step, so I'll **stop and show you a deletion manifest before removing anything**:

1. **Fingerprint** (read-only, now): size + head/tail partial hash of every file in a multi-copy group → cluster the true identical sets.
2. **Pick 2 keepers** per distinct archive (prefer copies sitting in *different* roots).
3. **Full hash the 2 keepers** and require they match — this *is* your validation, and it happens *before* any deletion, so we never delete until two good matching copies are confirmed.
4. **Present the deletion manifest** → you approve → then delete.

Let me start the read-only discovery phase.

content.text: Now I'll write the fingerprint script onto the NAS and run it (read-only).

input.command:
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'cat > /tmp/dedup_fp.sh' <<'SCRIPT'
#!/bin/bash
# Read-only: partial fingerprint (head 16M + tail 16M) of every file in a
# multi-copy (size,basename) group. Size is part of the group key already.
set -u
INV=/tmp/nas_inventory.tsv
ROOT=/volume1/homes/bhyslop
OUT=/tmp/fingerprints.tsv
cd "$ROOT" || exit 1
: > "$OUT"
# build list of (size,basename) keys with more than one member
awk -F'\t' '$2 ~ /7z$/ {c[$1"\t"$2]++} END{for(k in c) if(c[k]>1) print k}' "$INV" \
| while IFS=$'\t' read -r size base; do
    awk -F'\t' -v s="$size" -v b="$base" '$1==s && $2==b {print $3}' "$INV" \
    | while read -r p; do
        fp=$({ head -c 16777216 "$p"; tail -c 16777216 "$p"; } 2>/dev/null | md5sum | cut -d' ' -f1)
        printf '%s\t%s\t%s\t%s\n' "$size" "$base" "$fp" "$p" >> "$OUT"
        printf '.' >&2
      done
  done
echo >&2
echo "done: $(wc -l < "$OUT") files fingerprinted -> $OUT" >&2
SCRIPT
echo "script written"; ssh bhyslop-nas2 'wc -l /tmp/dedup_fp.sh'
input.description: Write fingerprint script to NAS

aiTitle: Deduplicate large password-protected files on NAS

content.text:
This is a genuinely important find — **it's not corruption, it's two different backups colliding on the same name and size**, split cleanly by source drive:

- `old-latitude-group.float2-1.7z`: the `SEAGATE_3TB` copies are one file; the `BLUE`/`SILVER` copies are a *different* file.
- `gauntlet-enclosure.1-selections.7z`: `SILVER` copies are one file; `SEAGATE_3TB`+`vault` copies are a *different* file.

A name+size dedup tool (or a careless human) would have deleted one of each as a "duplicate" and you'd have silently lost data. I'll treat each content-variant as its own archive and keep 2 of *each*.

Let me build the keep/delete manifest (keep 2 per distinct content, preferring copies in different top-level roots).

input.command:
cd /tmp; python3 - <<'PY'
import collections
rows=[]
with open('/tmp/fingerprints.tsv') as f:
    for line in f:
        size,base,fp,path=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t')
        rows.append((int(size),base,fp,path))
def root(p):  # top-level tree under ./
    return p.split('/')[1]
clusters=collections.defaultdict(list)
for size,base,fp,path in rows:
    clusters[(base,fp)].append((size,path))
VAULT='bhyslop-vault'
manifest=[]
for (base,fp),members in clusters.items():
    size=members[0][0]
    paths=[p for _,p in members]
    # rank: prefer vault, then alphabetical root for determinism
    def pri(p):
        r=root(p)
        return (0 if r==VAULT else 1, r, p)
    paths_sorted=sorted(paths,key=pri)
    keep=[paths_sorted[0]]
    # second keeper: first with a different top-level root
    for p in paths_sorted[1:]:
        if root(p)!=root(keep[0]):
            keep.append(p); break
    if len(keep)<2 and len(paths_sorted)>1:
        keep.append(paths_sorted[1])
    for p in paths_sorted:
        manifest.append(('KEEP' if p in keep else 'DELETE',size,base,fp[:8],p))
# write manifest
with open('/tmp/manifest.tsv','w') as o:
    for a,s,b,fp,p in sorted(manifest,key=lambda x:(x[2],x[3],x[0])):
        o.write(f"{a}\t{s}\t{b}\t{fp}\t{p}\n")
keep=[m for m in manifest if m[0]=='KEEP']
dele=[m for m in manifest if m[0]=='DELETE']
print(f"content clusters: {len(clusters)}")
print(f"KEEP:   {len(keep)} files, {sum(m[1] for m in keep)/1099511627776:.2f} TB")
print(f"DELETE: {len(dele)} files, {sum(m[1] for m in dele)/1099511627776:.2f} TB reclaimed")
print("\n=== KEEPERS (2 per distinct content) ===")
for a,s,b,fp,p in sorted(keep,key=lambda x:(x[2],x[3])):
    print(f"  {fp}  {s/1e9:7.1f}GB  {p}")
PY

content.text:
The manifest surfaced a **second class of landmine** worth your attention before anything gets deleted. The partial fingerprints reveal that several of your scary-sounding filenames are byte-identical to their clean namesakes:

| "Variant" file | Identical to | Same fingerprint |
|---|---|---|
| `...80G-drive.float1-1.broken1.7z` | `...80G-drive.float1-1.7z` | `bc93ecf2` |
| `20230207-float1-misc.broken1.7z` | `20230207-float1-misc.7z` | `dc213fdf` |
| `dazzler-...-1.broken.7z` **and** `.broken2.7z` | `dazzler-...-1.7z` | `310ce3ed` |
| `Corsair-240g-select.1.diffOnBbdr7z` | `Corsair-240g-select.1.7z` | `68d4849f` |

So `.broken`/`.broken2`/`.diffOnBbdr` are **mislabeled — the content is identical**. (`.REDO` on the gauntlet file is the exception: `eff28f26` is genuinely distinct.)

This forces one definitional choice that changes the outcome, and it's a judgment call I shouldn't make for you:

content.text: Decision locked: **collapse by content, keep 2, full-hash to confirm before deleting.** Let me compute the content-clustered plan and the minimal set of files to full-hash — every distinct *filename* gets at least one full hash (so cross-name identity is proven end-to-end), plus both keepers of each content.

input.command:
cd /tmp; python3 - <<'PY'
import collections
rows=[]
with open('/tmp/fingerprints.tsv') as f:
    for line in f:
        size,base,fp,path=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t')
        rows.append((int(size),base,fp,path))
def root(p): return p.split('/')[1]
VAULT='bhyslop-vault'
# content clusters keyed by (size, partial-fp)
clusters=collections.defaultdict(list)
for size,base,fp,path in rows:
    clusters[(size,fp)].append((base,path))
# keepers per cluster: prefer vault, then distinct top-level root
keepers=set()
cluster_keep={}
for key,members in clusters.items():
    paths=[p for _,p in members]
    paths_sorted=sorted(paths,key=lambda p:(0 if root(p)==VAULT else 1, root(p), p))
    keep=[paths_sorted[0]]
    for p in paths_sorted[1:]:
        if root(p)!=root(keep[0]): keep.append(p); break
    if len(keep)<2 and len(paths_sorted)>1: keep.append(paths_sorted[1])
    cluster_keep[key]=keep
    keepers.update(keep)
# one full-hash representative per distinct (size,basename) NAME
name_rep={}
for size,base,fp,path in rows:
    nk=(size,base)
    # prefer a path that's a keeper, else first seen (stable: prefer vault/sorted)
    if nk not in name_rep:
        name_rep[nk]=path
    if path in keepers:
        name_rep[nk]=path
fullhash=set(keepers)|set(name_rep.values())
# size of distinct content
unique=sum(s for (s,fp) in clusters)
deleted=[p for _,p in (m for ms in clusters.values() for m in ms) if p not in keepers]
del_bytes=0; keep_bytes=0
allmembers=[(s,p) for (s,fp),ms in clusters.items() for (b,p) in ms]
for s,p in allmembers:
    if p in keepers: keep_bytes+=s
    else: del_bytes+=s
fh_bytes=0
for (s,fp),ms in clusters.items():
    for b,p in ms:
        if p in fullhash: fh_bytes+=s
with open('/tmp/fullhash_list.txt','w') as o:
    for p in sorted(fullhash): o.write(p+'\n')
# stash cluster_keep for later manifest build
import json
json.dump({f"{k[0]}|{k[1]}":v for k,v in cluster_keep.items()}, open('/tmp/cluster_keep.json','w'))
print(f"distinct content clusters : {len(clusters)}")
print(f"distinct filenames         : {len(name_rep)}")
print(f"KEEP   (2 per content)     : {len(keepers)} files, {keep_bytes/1099511627776:.2f} TB")
print(f"DELETE                     : {len(allmembers)-len(keepers)} files, {del_bytes/1099511627776:.2f} TB reclaimed")
print(f"FILES TO FULL-HASH now     : {len(fullhash)} files, {fh_bytes/1099511627776:.2f} TB to read (~{fh_bytes/200e6/3600:.1f}h @200MB/s)")
PY

2. **My earlier "18 TB on an 8.8 TB volume → reflinks" explanation was wrong.** The real reason the totals didn't add up is that ~half those files live on the *other* NAS. Let me recompute local-vs-remote and correct the record.

content.text:
Now the picture is correct and actually simpler:

- **Local btrfs volume: 129 files, 8.52 TB** — that genuinely fills your 8.8 TB volume (the 98%-full is real, and these *are* real physical duplicates, not extent-shared — so deleting redundant local copies frees real space).
- **Second NAS (bobbie/, SMB): 148 files, 9.80 TB — untouched, off-limits.**

The bobbie copies are still useful as *information* (they tell you a given archive is also backed up on the other box), but they will not be deleted, not modified, and not counted as one of your "2 kept" — the dedup operates **purely on local files**. Let me rebuild the plan local-only.

input.command:
cd /tmp; python3 - <<'PY'
import collections,json
rows=[]
with open('/tmp/fingerprints.tsv') as f:
    for line in f:
        size,base,fp,path=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t'); rows.append((int(size),base,fp,path))
# include singletons (not fingerprinted) too, from inventory, so we account for all local 7z
fp_by_path={p:fp for s,b,fp,p in rows}
allrows=[]
with open('/tmp/nas_inventory.tsv') as f:
    for line in f:
        s,b,p=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t')
        if not b.endswith('7z'): continue
        allrows.append((int(s),b,fp_by_path.get(p,'SINGLETON-'+p),p))
def remote(p): return p.startswith('./bobbie/')
def root(p): return p.split('/')[1]
VAULT='bhyslop-vault'
clusters=collections.defaultdict(list)
for s,b,fp,p in allrows: clusters[(s,fp)].append((b,p))
keepers=set(); deletes=[]; cluster_rows=[]
for key,members in clusters.items():
    s=key[0]
    local=[p for _,p in members if not remote(p)]
    rem=[p for _,p in members if remote(p)]
    ls=sorted(local,key=lambda p:(0 if root(p)==VAULT else 1, root(p), p))
    keep=[]
    if ls:
        keep=[ls[0]]
        for p in ls[1:]:
            if root(p)!=root(keep[0]): keep.append(p); break
        if len(keep)<2 and len(ls)>1: keep.append(ls[1])
    keepers.update(keep)
    for p in ls:
        if p not in keep: deletes.append((s,p))
    cluster_rows.append((s,len(local),len(rem),len(keep),len(local)-len(keep)))
keep_bytes=sum(s for (s,fp),ms in clusters.items() for b,p in ms if p in keepers)
del_bytes=sum(s for s,p in deletes)
# full-hash set: keepers + one local rep per (size,basename) local name
name_rep={}
for s,b,fp,p in allrows:
    if remote(p): continue
    nk=(s,b)
    if nk not in name_rep: name_rep[nk]=p
    if p in keepers: name_rep[nk]=p
fullhash=sorted(set(keepers)|set(name_rep.values()))
fh_bytes=sum(s for (s,fp),ms in clusters.items() for b,p in ms if p in fullhash)
open('/tmp/fullhash_local.txt','w').write('\n'.join(fullhash)+'\n')
open('/tmp/delete_local.txt','w').write('\n'.join(p for _,p in sorted(deletes))+'\n')
# clusters with <2 local copies (rely on bobbie for offsite redundancy)
thin=[(s,nl,nr) for s,nl,nr,nk,nd in cluster_rows if nl<2]
print(f"content clusters (local+remote): {len(clusters)}")
print(f"LOCAL KEEP (<=2 per content): {len(keepers)} files, {keep_bytes/1099511627776:.2f} TB")
print(f"LOCAL DELETE                : {len(deletes)} files, {del_bytes/1099511627776:.2f} TB freed on local volume")
print(f"FULL-HASH (local only)      : {len(fullhash)} files, {fh_bytes/1099511627776:.2f} TB read (~{fh_bytes/200e6/3600:.1f}h, no network)")
print(f"\nclusters with <2 LOCAL copies (only {0} means fine; these lean on bobbie/other-NAS): {len(thin)}")
for s,nl,nr in thin: print(f"   {s/1e9:7.1f}GB  local={nl} remote(bobbie)={nr}")
PY

content.text:
Here's the corrected, **local-only** plan. Nothing touches the second NAS.

## Revised numbers (local btrfs only)

| | files | size |
|---|---|---|
| **Keep** (≤2 local copies per distinct content) | 41 | 2.64 TB |
| **Delete** (redundant local copies) | 85 | **5.87 TB freed** |
| Full-hash to validate first (local, no network) | 46 | 3.04 TB (~4–5 h) |

Deleting 5.87 TB takes the local volume from **98% full → ~31%**. The 148 bobbie/ files (9.8 TB on the other NAS) are never read again, never modified, never deleted.

## One thing to flag

There's a 13.1 GB local file with **no second copy anywhere** — not locally, not on bobbie:
`0230207-old-latitude-group.float1-xxx.7z` (note the typo'd name, missing leading `2`). Its *size* matches the `...float1-1.7z` family, so it may actually be the same content under a fat-fingered name. It's in the **keep** set regardless; I'll fingerprint it during validation to tell you whether it's truly unique or just a misnamed twin.

## Corrected answer to your md5 puzzle

My first explanation was half-wrong once the SMB mount came to light. The accurate version:

- **md5 is slow because it's CPU-bound on a weak core.** A DS223j is a quad Cortex-A55 with no crypto acceleration; `md5sum` is single-threaded and must read *every byte* — ~150–250 MB/s ceiling. A 191 GB archive = 15–20 min of pure grind.
- **"Copying feels fast" for one of two reasons:** (a) same-volume local copies on btrfs use **reflink** — copy-on-write, zero bytes moved, instant; or (b) the copy wasn't CPU-bound the way hashing is. Either way, the bottleneck for hashing is the CPU, and for copying it usually isn't.
- For the bobbie files specifically, *both* operations also cross gigabit SMB (~110 MB/s) — which is partly why my head/tail fingerprinting of those took a while.
- The "real" fix (xxHash/BLAKE3, 5–20× faster, disk-bound) isn't installed and a DS223j is awkward to add it to — but **the bigger win is not hashing 18 TB at all**: dedup needs no hashing of the 9.8 TB you're keeping on bobbie, and only ~3 TB of local reads validates the survivors.

## Next step

The validation run is **read-only, local files only**, ~4–5 hours. It full-hashes the 41 keepers + one representative of each distinct local filename, so every cross-name identity (e.g. `.broken2` == clean) is proven end-to-end *before* I generate the final deletion manifest. **No deletions happen until you approve that manifest.**

Want me to launch the validation hash run in the background now?

message.content: yes, launch the validation run in the background

content.text: I'll push the file list, write the validation script, and launch it detached on the NAS so it survives the SSH session.

input.command:
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'cat > /tmp/fullhash_local.txt' < /tmp/fullhash_local.txt
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'cat > /tmp/validate.sh' <<'SCRIPT'
#!/bin/bash
# Read-only validation: full md5 of each LOCAL file in the list, sequential.
set -u
ROOT=/volume1/homes/bhyslop
LIST=/tmp/fullhash_local.txt
OUT=/tmp/fullhash_results.tsv
DONE=/tmp/validate.done
cd "$ROOT" || exit 1
: > "$OUT"
total=$(grep -c . "$LIST")
i=0
while IFS= read -r p; do
  [ -z "$p" ] && continue
  case "$p" in ./bobbie/*) echo "REFUSED-REMOTE $p" >> "$OUT"; continue;; esac  # safety: never SMB
  i=$((i+1))
  start=$(date +%s)
  md5=$(md5sum "$p" 2>/dev/null | cut -d' ' -f1)
  sz=$(stat -c%s "$p" 2>/dev/null)
  end=$(date +%s)
  printf '%s\t%s\t%s\n' "$md5" "$sz" "$p" >> "$OUT"
  printf '[%d/%d] %ds %s\n' "$i" "$total" "$((end-start))" "$p" >> /tmp/validate.progress
done < "$LIST"
echo "completed $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ), $i files" > "$DONE"
SCRIPT
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'rm -f /tmp/validate.done /tmp/validate.progress; nohup bash /tmp/validate.sh >/tmp/validate.log 2>&1 </dev/null & echo "launched pid $!"; wc -l /tmp/fullhash_local.txt'

Launched and confirmed running — PID 26316, currently grinding the first file at 99% CPU (the typo'd `float1-xxx.7z`, which lives in `bhyslop-vault/`, so local). The script has a hard safety guard that **refuses any `./bobbie/` path** (writes `REFUSED-REMOTE` instead of hashing), so even a list error can't reach the second NAS.

**What it's doing:** full md5 of 46 local files (~3 TB), sequential, read-only, no network. ETA ~4–5 hours.

**Progress is tracked in three files on the NAS:**
- `/tmp/validate.progress` — one line per completed file with per-file timing
- `/tmp/fullhash_results.tsv` — `md5 ⟶ size ⟶ path` as each finishes
- `/tmp/validate.done` — written only when the whole run completes

Since this runs on the NAS (not something this session auto-watches), it won't ping me on its own. Just say **"check the validation"** anytime and I'll pull progress. When it finishes I'll:

1. Verify the 2 keepers of each content cluster have **matching** md5s (the actual validation gate).
2. Confirm the cross-name identities (`.broken2` == clean, `.diffOnBbdr` == clean, etc.) end-to-end.
3. Resolve whether `float1-xxx.7z` is truly unique or a misnamed twin of `float1-1`.
4. Generate the final **deletion manifest** (85 files, 5.87 TB) and show it to you. **Nothing gets deleted until you approve it.**

40	## Working Preferences
41	- When user mentions an acronym, immediately navigate to the corresponding file
42	- Assume full edit permissions for all files in the three main directories
43	- For bash scripts, prefer functional programming style with clear error handling
44	- For .adoc files, maintain consistent AsciiDoc formatting
45	- For .claudex files, preserve the specific format requirements
46	
47	### Heredoc Delimiter Selection
48	
49	When generating heredocs for stdin content, the delimiter must not appear alone on any line within the content.
50	
51	- **Check content first**: If content includes `EOF` (e.g., code examples showing heredoc patterns), use a different delimiter
52	- **Safe alternatives**: `SPEC`, `CONTENT`, `DOC`, `PACESPEC`, `SLASHCMD`
53	- **Pattern**: `cat <<'DELIM' | command` (quoted delimiter prevents variable expansion)
54	
55	### AsciiDoc Linked Terms
56	When working with .adoc files using MCM patterns:
57	- **Linked Term**: Concept with three parts:
58	  - Attribute reference: `:prefix_snake_case:` (mapping section)
59	  - Replacement text: `<<anchor,Display Text>>` (what readers see)
60	  - Definition: `[[anchor]] {attribute}:: Definition text` (meaning)
61	- Definitions may be grouped in lists or dispersed through document
62	- Maintain consistent prefix categories (e.g., `mcm_`, `rbw_`, `gad_`)
63	- Use snake_case for anchors, match attribute to anchor
64	
65	### Rust Build Discipline
66	
67	Two orthogonal Rust pipelines. Always use the tabtarget, never raw cargo commands.
68	
69	**VOW pipeline** (vvk/jjk/cmk kits — parceled for delivery):
70	- `tt/vow-b.Build.sh` — build vvr binary and install to VVK bin
71	- `tt/vow-t.Test.sh` — run all kit crate tests
72	- `tt/vvw-r.RunVVX.sh <cmd>` — run vvx binary with arguments
73	
74	**RBTW pipeline** (theurge — rbk's own test infrastructure, orthogonal from VOW):
75	- `tt/rbtd-b.Build.sh` — build theurge crate
76	- `tt/rbtd-t.Test.sh` — run theurge unit tests
77	
78	### Test Execution
79	
80	**Test suites** group fixtures by dependency tier. Run the broadest applicable suite:
81	
82	| Suite | Tabtarget | Dependencies | What it covers |
83	|-------|-----------|-------------|----------------|
84	| `fast` | `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh` | None | enrollment-validation (47), regime-validation (21), regime-smoke (7) = 75 cases |
85	| `service` | `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.service.sh` | GCP credentials | fast + access-probe (4), hallmark-lifecycle (1), batch-vouch (1) = 81 cases |
86	| `crucible` | `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.crucible.sh` | Container runtime | fast + tadmor-security (34), srjcl-jupyter (3), pluml-diagram (5) = 117 cases |
87	| `complete` | `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.complete.sh` | All of the above | All 8 fixtures = 122 cases |
88	
89	**After code changes**, run the appropriate tier:
90	- Regime/validation changes → `fast`
91	- Foundry/credential changes → `service`
92	- Bottle/sentry/network changes → `crucible`
93	- Pre-release or decomposition sweep → `complete`
94	
95	**Single fixture**: `tt/rbtd-r.FixtureRun.{name}.sh` (e.g., `tadmor`, `enrollment-validation`, `regime-smoke`)
96	
97	**Single case**: `tt/rbtd-s.FixtureCase.sh <fixture> [case-name]` — run one case against an already-charged crucible (no charge/quench). Omit case name to list all cases for the fixture; omit fixture to list all fixtures. Workflow for crucible debugging: charge via `tt/rbw-cC.Charge.{nameplate}.sh`, run individual cases, quench via `tt/rbw-cQ.Quench.{nameplate}.sh` when done.
98	
99	**BUK self-test**: `tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh` — exercises BUK test framework (kick-tires + bure-tweak, 9 cases)
100	
101	**Sequential only**: Never run fixtures in parallel — they share regime state and container namespaces.

103	## Prefix Naming Discipline ("mint")
104	
105	When asked to "mint" names, apply these rules. Full study: `Memos/memo-20260110-acronym-selection-study.md`
106	
107	### Two Universes
108	
109	**Primary Universe** — ANY persistent identifier, regardless of where it lives. This includes the obvious (code, docs, functions, variables, directories) AND the easy-to-miss: git refs, slash commands, environment variables, paths in target repos, configuration keys. **If it's a name that persists, it's in scope.** Prefixes must be globally unique and respect terminal exclusivity.
110	
111	**Tabtarget Universe** — launchers in `tt/`. These are *colophons* referencing the primary universe. `rbw-` points to the `rbw` workbench; it doesn't consume new prefix space.
112	
113	### Core Rules
114	
115	**Rule 1 - Project Prefix**: Names start with 2-4 char project ID:
116	`rb` (Recipe Bottle), `gad` (GAD), `bu` (BUK), `jj` (Job Jockey), `pb` (Paneboard), `mcm`/`axl` (CMK), `crg`, `wrs`, `hm` (HMK), `lmci`, `vsl` (VSLK)
117	
118	**Rule 2 - Terminal Exclusivity**: A prefix either IS a name or HAS children, never both.
119	- `rbg` has children (`rbga`, `rbgb`...) → `rbg` cannot name a thing
120	- `rbi` names Image module → `rbia`, `rbib` forbidden
121	
122	### Primary Universe Patterns
123	
124	| Domain | Pattern | Example |
125	|--------|---------|---------|
126	| Code files | `prefix_Word.ext` | `rbga_ArtifactRegistry.sh` |
127	| Doc files | `ACRONYM-Words.ext` | `RBS0-SpecTop.adoc` |
128	| Functions (public) | `prefix_name()` | `buc_log_args()` |
129	| Functions (private) | `zprefix_name()` | `zbuc_color()` |
130	| Variables | `PREFIX_NAME` | `BURC_PROJECT_ROOT` |
131	| AsciiDoc attributes | `:prefix_term:` | `:rbw_depot:` |
132	| AsciiDoc anchors | `[[prefix_term]]` | `[[rbw_depot]]` |
133	| Directories | `prefix/` | `Tools/buk/` |
134	
135	### Tabtarget Universe Pattern
136	
137	Tabtargets follow: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
138	
139	Colophons must reference valid Primary Universe prefixes. See **BUK Concepts** in the BUK include for terminology (colophon, frontispiece, imprint, workbench).
140	
141	Tabtargets dispatch through `tt/z-launcher.sh` by passing a moorings-launcher *sprue* `{owner}ml_{launcher-id}` (`rbml_*` RBK-authored, `buml_*` BUK-hosted) — an underscore-universe dispatch token kept distinct from the hyphenated colophon. See BCG "Tabtarget Path Indirection" for the rationale.
142	
143	### Extended Namespace Checklist
144	
145	When minting, enumerate ALL namespaces the system touches:
146	
147	| Namespace | Pattern | Example |
148	|-----------|---------|---------|
149	| Git refs | `refs/{prefix}/...` | `refs/vvg/locks/*` |

19	## Bash Utility Kit (BUK) Concepts
20	
21	BUK provides tabtarget/launcher infrastructure for bash-based tooling.
22	
23	### TabTarget System
24	
25	TabTargets are lightweight shell scripts in `tt/` that serve as the CLI entry point for all operations. They delegate to workbenches via launchers — no business logic lives in tabtargets.
26	
27	**Discoverability**: `ls tt/` shows all available commands. Tab completion narrows by prefix: `tt/rbw-<TAB>`.
28	
29	**Naming pattern**: `{colophon}.{frontispiece}[.{imprint}].sh`
30	
31	| Part | Purpose | Example |
32	|------|---------|---------|
33	| **Colophon** | Routing identifier (includes hyphen, workbench matches on this) | `rbw-cr`, `buw-tt-ll` |
34	| **Frontispiece** | Human-readable description (PascalCase) | `Rack` |
35	| **Imprint** | Optional target parameter (nameplate moniker, fixture name, etc.) | `tadmor` |
36	
37	- The `.` is the delimiter between parts
38	- The hyphen is part of the colophon (not a separator)
39	
40	Example: `tt/rbw-cr.Rack.tadmor.sh` — colophon `rbw-cr` routes to the crucible rack command, frontispiece tells you what it does, imprint `tadmor` selects the nameplate.
41	
42	Multiple tabtargets can share the same colophon but differ by imprint:
43	```
44	tt/rbw-cC.Charge.tadmor.sh
45	tt/rbw-cC.Charge.srjcl.sh
46	tt/rbw-cC.Charge.pluml.sh
47	```
48	
49	### BUK Vocabulary
50	
51	| Term | Definition |
52	|------|------------|
53	| **Zipper** | BCG-compliant module kindling colophon→module→command array constants |
54	| **Workbench** | Routes commands: `{prefix}w_workbench.sh` |
55	| **Testbench** | Routes tests: `{prefix}t_testbench.sh` |
56	| **Folio** | Runtime target value (`BUZ_FOLIO`) passed to a command — nameplate moniker, role name, etc. How it arrives depends on the channel |
57	| **Channel** | Enrollment-time declaration of how a colophon receives its folio: `imprint` (from filename — one tabtarget per target), `param1` (command-line argument — single tabtarget), or empty (no folio needed) |
58	
59	**Key files:**
60	- `Tools/buk/buc_command.sh` — command utilities
61	- `Tools/buk/bud_dispatch.sh` — dispatch utilities
62	- `Tools/buk/buw_workbench.sh` — workbench
63	
64	Full spec: `Tools/buk/README.md`
65	
66	## Forbidden Shell Operations
67	
68	**Never use `cd` in Bash commands — NO exceptions.**
69	
70	The working directory persists between Bash tool calls. A single `cd` corrupts ALL subsequent commands that use relative paths, including every `./tt/` tabtarget.
71	
72	- Use absolute paths instead of cd'ing
73	
74	**There is no safe cd.** Do not reason that "I'll cd back" — the next tool call may be yours or another Claude Code session's, and it will break.
75	
76	## TabTarget Invocation Discipline
77	
78	**Never wrap tabtarget invocations with `tee`, `tail`, `head`, `grep`, `2>&1`, or any other pipe — NO exceptions.**
79	
80	Tabtargets self-log to `../logs-buk/`:
81	- `last.txt` — most recent invocation across all tabtargets
82	- `same-{cmd}.txt` — most recent invocation of this specific tabtarget
83	- `hist-{cmd}-{timestamp}.txt` — historical invocations
84	
85	Both stdout and stderr are captured. Adding your own `tee` or `2>&1` duplicates work the tool already did. The real hazard is piping a tabtarget into `tail`/`head`/`grep`: zsh defaults `pipefail` OFF, so the pipeline returns the last command's exit status — usually 0. **A failing test reports as success.**
86	
87	- Run the tabtarget directly, then read from `../logs-buk/` in a separate command
88	- Environment variables before the command are fine: `BURE_CONFIRM=skip ./tt/foo.sh`
89	- If you genuinely must pipe live output (rare — usually you can read the log instead), set `-o pipefail` on the same line, or check `${PIPESTATUS[0]}` (bash) / `${pipestatus[1]}` (zsh)
90	
91	```
92	# Wrong — exit code is from `tail`, not the tabtarget; failures masked
93	./tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh 2>&1 | tee /tmp/log | tail -80
94	
95	# Wrong — even a bare `| head` discards the real signal
96	./tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh | head -50
97	
98	# Right — separate commands; exit code preserved
99	./tt/rbw-tP.QualifyPristine.sh
100	tail -80 ../logs-buk/last.txt
101	```
102	
103	**There is no safe `tee | tail`.** Do not reason "I'll just truncate the output for readability" — the truncation and the exit-code-eating are inseparable. Truncate by reading the log file afterward.
104	
105	## Test Execution Discipline
106	
107	Run test fixture tabtargets **sequentially, never in parallel**. Test fixtures share regime state and container/network namespaces — parallel execution causes resource conflicts and false failures.
108	
109	```
110	# Correct: run one at a time
111	tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.regime-validation.sh
112	tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.tadmor-security.sh
113	
114	# Wrong: never run fixtures concurrently
115	tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.regime-validation.sh & tt/rbw-tf.TestFixture.tadmor-security.sh &
116	```

Nearly done — **43 of 46 files hashed**, 3 remaining (currently on `dazzler-...broken2.7z`, ~13 min, then two more). Local reads are running well — ~175–180 MB/s, faster than my 200 MB/s-pessimistic estimate, so the full run is landing in roughly ~2 hours rather than 4–5.

A couple of things are already visible in the timings worth noting:

- The **two divergent-content files** I flagged earlier are confirmed cheap to tell apart — both `old-latitude-group.float2-1.7z` copies (SEAGATE source) hashed (`[38]` 69s, `[42]` 80s); their content hashes will be compared against the BLUE/SILVER variant.
- The **`.broken`/`.broken2`/`.diffOnBbdr` files are being fully read** (`[36]` diffOnBbdr, `[37]` broken1, `[43]` broken, and broken2 now) — these are exactly the cross-name identity claims, now being proven end-to-end against their clean twins.

Once `validate.done` appears, I'll run the cross-check and produce:
1. Pass/fail on each content cluster's 2 keepers matching.
2. Confirmed verdict on every `.broken*`/`.diffOnBbdr` == clean (or not).
3. The `float1-xxx.7z` unique-or-twin answer.
4. The final deletion manifest for your approval.

Say **"check"** in ~15 minutes (or whenever) and I'll finalize. Nothing deletes without your sign-off.

input.command:
cd /tmp; python3 - <<'PY'
import collections,json
# full md5 results
full={}   # path -> (md5,size)
for line in open('/tmp/fullhash_results.tsv'):
    md5,size,path=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t'); full[path]=(md5,int(size))
# partial fingerprints + inventory (local 7z only)
part={}
for line in open('/tmp/fingerprints.tsv'):
    s,b,fp,p=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t'); part[p]=(int(s),b,fp)
local=[]
for line in open('/tmp/nas_inventory.tsv'):
    s,b,p=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t')
    if b.endswith('7z') and not p.startswith('./bobbie/'): local.append((int(s),b,p))

print("="*70)
print("1) CONTENT IDENTITY BY FULL md5 (names that are byte-identical)")
print("="*70)
bymd5=collections.defaultdict(set)
for p,(m,s) in full.items():
    bymd5[(s,m)].add(part.get(p,(s,'?',''))[1])
for (s,m),names in sorted(bymd5.items(),key=lambda x:-x[0][0]):
    if len(names)>1:
        print(f"  {m[:12]} {s/1e9:7.1f}GB  <=  {'  ==  '.join(sorted(names))}")
print("  (single-name md5s omitted)")

print("\n"+"="*70)
print("2) DIVERGENT NAMES CONFIRMED (same name+size, DIFFERENT md5)")
print("="*70)
name2md5=collections.defaultdict(set)
for p,(m,s) in full.items():
    name2md5[(s,part.get(p,(s,'?',''))[1])].add(m)
for (s,b),ms in sorted(name2md5.items()):
    if len(ms)>1:
        print(f"  {b} ({s/1e9:.1f}GB): {len(ms)} distinct contents -> {sorted(x[:8] for x in ms)}")

print("\n"+"="*70)
print("3) float1-xxx.7z : unique or misnamed twin?")
print("="*70)
xxx=[(p,full[p]) for p in full if 'float1-xxx' in p]
for p,(m,s) in xxx:
    twins=[part[q][1] for q in full if full[q][0]==m and 'float1-xxx' not in q]
    print(f"  {p}\n    md5={m[:16]} size={s/1e9:.1f}GB")
    print(f"    matches: {twins if twins else 'NOTHING — genuinely unique, NO second copy anywhere'}")

print("\n"+"="*70)
print("4) KEEPER PAIR CHECK — do the 2 retained copies of each content match?")
print("="*70)
ck=json.load(open('/tmp/cluster_keep.json'))
allmatch=True
for key,keep in ck.items():
    keep=[k for k in keep if not k.startswith('./bobbie/')]
    md5s={full[k][0] for k in keep if k in full}
    if len(keep)>=2:
        ok = len(md5s)==1
        allmatch &= ok
        if not ok:
            print(f"  MISMATCH {key[:20]}: {[(k,full[k][0][:8]) for k in keep]}")
print("  ALL keeper pairs match ✓" if allmatch else "  *** MISMATCHES ABOVE — do not delete ***")
PY

content.text:
This is the critical catch — and it's exactly why we full-hashed before deleting. **The partial fingerprint was wrong.** Every `.broken`/`.broken2`/`.broken1`/`.diffOnBbdr`/`.REDO` file is **genuinely different content** from its clean twin — they share the same 7z header (first 16 MB) and trailer (last 16 MB) but **differ in the middle**. That's the textbook signature of a partial/corrupted copy: archive structure intact at the ends, payload divergent in the body.

In other words: **the suffixes were telling the truth.** Had we trusted the head+tail fingerprint and "collapsed by content," we would have deleted real, distinct data. The validation step did its job.

Two consequences:

1. **The "collapse by content" decision is now void** — those files are not duplicates. Each distinct filename here tracks distinct content, so we're effectively back to *keep each distinct archive*, and the `.broken*` variants are their own archives (likely failed/partial backup attempts you kept deliberately).
2. **Partial hashing is disqualified for *any* deletion decision on this data.** If head+tail collided across these files, I can't trust it within the same-name copy groups either. The only safe dedup key is a **full md5**.

So the safe path is: **full-hash every local copy** (not just representatives), then delete only files whose *full* md5 exactly matches a retained copy. That also doubles as bit-rot detection — if two copies that should be identical differ, that's corruption to investigate, not delete.

local=[]
for line in open('/tmp/nas_inventory.tsv'):
    s,b,p=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t')
    if b.endswith('7z') and not p.startswith('./bobbie/'): local.append((int(s),p))
remaining=[(s,p) for s,p in local if p not in done]
rb=sum(s for s,p in remaining)
print(f"local 7z files total : {len(local)} ({sum(s for s,_ in local)/1099511627776:.2f} TB)")
print(f"already full-hashed  : {len(done & {p for _,p in local})}")
print(f"REMAINING to hash    : {len(remaining)} files, {rb/1099511627776:.2f} TB  (~{rb/175e6/3600:.1f}h @175MB/s)")
open('/tmp/remaining_local.txt','w').write('\n'.join(p for _,p in sorted(remaining))+'\n')
print("wrote /tmp/remaining_local.txt")
PY

input.command:
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'cat > /tmp/remaining_local.txt' < /tmp/remaining_local.txt
# new script appends to the SAME results file so all 126 land together; same REFUSED-REMOTE guard
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'cat > /tmp/validate2.sh' <<'SCRIPT'
#!/bin/bash
set -u
ROOT=/volume1/homes/bhyslop
LIST=/tmp/remaining_local.txt
OUT=/tmp/fullhash_results.tsv      # append; keepers already present
DONE=/tmp/validate2.done
cd "$ROOT" || exit 1
total=$(grep -c . "$LIST"); i=0
while IFS= read -r p; do
  [ -z "$p" ] && continue
  case "$p" in ./bobbie/*) echo "REFUSED-REMOTE $p" >> "$OUT"; continue;; esac
  i=$((i+1)); start=$(date +%s)
  md5=$(md5sum "$p" 2>/dev/null | cut -d' ' -f1)
  sz=$(stat -c%s "$p" 2>/dev/null)
  end=$(date +%s)
  printf '%s\t%s\t%s\n' "$md5" "$sz" "$p" >> "$OUT"
  printf '[%d/%d] %ds %s\n' "$i" "$total" "$((end-start))" "$p" >> /tmp/validate2.progress
done < "$LIST"
echo "completed $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ), $i files" > "$DONE"
SCRIPT
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'rm -f /tmp/validate2.done /tmp/validate2.progress; nohup bash /tmp/validate2.sh >/tmp/validate2.log 2>&1 </dev/null & echo "launched pid $!"; echo "list: $(wc -l < /tmp/remaining_local.txt) files"'

input.command:
cd /tmp; python3 - <<'PY'
import collections,json
full={}
for line in open('/tmp/fullhash_results.tsv'):
    md5,size,path=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t'); full[path]=(md5,int(size))
def root(p): return p.split('/')[1]
def bname(p): return p.split('/')[-1]
VAULT='bhyslop-vault'

# (A) corruption check: same (size, basename) but differing md5 among copies
byname=collections.defaultdict(set)
namepaths=collections.defaultdict(list)
for p,(m,s) in full.items():
    byname[(s,bname(p))].add(m); namepaths[(s,bname(p))].append(p)
print("="*72); print("(A) SAME-NAME COPIES THAT DISAGREE (candidate corruption / divergent capture)"); print("="*72)
corrupt=0
for (s,b),ms in sorted(byname.items()):
    if len(ms)>1:
        corrupt+=1
        print(f"  {b} ({s/1e9:.1f}GB): {len(ms)} distinct md5 across {len(namepaths[(s,b)])} copies")
        groups=collections.defaultdict(list)
        for p in namepaths[(s,b)]: groups[full[p][0]].append(p)
        for m,ps in groups.items():
            print(f"      {m[:12]}  x{len(ps)}")
            for p in ps: print(f"          {p}")
print("  none" if corrupt==0 else f"  >>> {corrupt} name(s) with divergent content — handle as DISTINCT, do not cross-delete")

# (B) exact-md5 dedup: cluster by full md5, keep 2 (prefer vault, then distinct root)
clusters=collections.defaultdict(list)
for p,(m,s) in full.items(): clusters[m].append(p)
keepers=set(); deletes=[]
for m,paths in clusters.items():
    ps=sorted(paths,key=lambda p:(0 if root(p)==VAULT else 1, root(p), p))
    keep=[ps[0]]
    for p in ps[1:]:
        if root(p)!=root(keep[0]): keep.append(p); break
    if len(keep)<2 and len(ps)>1: keep.append(ps[1])
    keepers.update(keep)
    for p in ps:
        if p not in keep: deletes.append(p)
keepB=sum(full[p][1] for p in keepers); delB=sum(full[p][1] for p in deletes)
print("\n"+"="*72); print("(B) EXACT-md5 DEDUP PLAN (keep 2 per distinct content)"); print("="*72)
print(f"  distinct contents (md5)   : {len(clusters)}")
print(f"  KEEP                      : {len(keepers)} files, {keepB/1099511627776:.2f} TB")
print(f"  DELETE                    : {len(deletes)} files, {delB/1099511627776:.2f} TB freed")
print(f"  clusters with only 1 copy : {sum(1 for m,ps in clusters.items() if len(ps)==1)} (kept, nothing to delete)")
open('/tmp/delete_final.txt','w').write('\n'.join(sorted(deletes))+'\n')
json.dump({m:[p for p in keepers if full[p][0]==m] for m in clusters}, open('/tmp/keepers_final.json','w'))
print("  wrote /tmp/delete_final.txt + /tmp/keepers_final.json")
PY

content.text:
Yes — NAS is reachable and the run completed cleanly yesterday (12:42 UTC). All 126 local files now have full md5s, and the exact-hash analysis surfaced **more of the same landmine** — which fully vindicates the decision to hash everything.

## Key findings

**4 archives have same-name/same-size copies with genuinely different content**, and the split tracks the *source drive* every time — `FROM_SEAGATE_3TB` holds one version, `FROM_SILVER`/`FROM_BLUE` holds another:

| Archive | Size | Variant A | Variant B |
|---|---|---|---|
| `old-latitude-group.float2-1.7z` | 12.3 GB | `f072052d` ×4 (BLUE/SILVER/vault) | `02ab1f7b` ×3 (SEAGATE) |
| `old-latitude-group.float1-1.7z` | 13.1 GB | `5a7efa8e` ×3 (BLUE/SILVER) | `197c4853` ×2 (SEAGATE) |
| `gauntlet-enclosure.1-selections.7z` | 73.8 GB | `955457d7` ×4 (SEAGATE/vault) | `028ffba5` ×2 (SILVER) |
| `horde-black-seagate-usb.2.7z` | **191.4 GB** | `d041e999` ×3 (SEAGATE) | `67976498` ×2 (SILVER) |

These are **two distinct backup generations stored under one name** — not corruption. The plan keeps 2 of *each* variant, so nothing is lost. A name+size dedup would have silently destroyed one version of each, including 191 GB.

**The `float1-xxx.7z` mystery is solved (good news):** its md5 is `5a7efa8e` — identical to the BLUE/SILVER `float1-1.7z`. It's not unique at all; it's a **misnamed 4th copy**. So it dedups normally and you lose nothing keeping 2.

## Final plan (exact md5, keep 2 per distinct content)

- **27 distinct contents**, every one has ≥2 local copies (nothing is a lone original).
- **KEEP: 54 files / 3.80 TB** · **DELETE: 72 files / 4.72 TB**
- Local volume: **98% full → ~44%**.

(Lower than the 5.87 TB upper bound — exactly because the `.broken*`/divergent variants turned out distinct and are retained.)

Let me generate the full deletion manifest for your review.

The full manifest is above — every one of the 72 deletions is a file whose **exact md5 matches two retained copies**, and every distinct content (including all the divergent variants) keeps 2.

**Summary:** 27 distinct contents · keep 54 (3.80 TB) · **delete 72 (4.72 TB)** · volume 98% → ~44%.

Two caveats worth your eye before I touch anything:

- **`float1-xxx.7z` keeper pairing** (`5a7efa8e`): the misnamed vault file is kept alongside `FROM_BLUE--1/.../float1-1.7z`. Same bytes, different names — that's fine, but if you'd rather I keep the *correctly-named* copy in vault's slot, say so.
- **SEAGATE-only variants** (`197c4853`, `02ab1f7b`, etc.): their only copies live under `fromdan/fromdan2/`, so both keepers sit in the same tree (less independent than the vault+BLUE pairs). The off-box `bobbie/` NAS very likely holds further copies, but I haven't verified that — want me to confirm bobbie coverage for those before deleting their local siblings?

Nothing is deleted yet. How do you want to proceed?

options.label: Quarantine then delete (Recommended)
options.description: Move the 72 files into a _TO_DELETE/ dir first (instant rename, same volume). You spot-check, then I rm the dir. Reversible until the final purge.

options.label: Delete directly now
options.description: rm the 72 files immediately. Frees 4.72 TB at once. Irreversible (relies on your bobbie/offsite copies).

options.label: Give me the script
options.description: I generate a reviewed rm script (with the exact md5-verified paths) and you run it yourself when ready. I don't delete anything.

options.label: Hold — verify bobbie first
options.description: Before any deletion, I check the second NAS for coverage of the SEAGATE-only variants, so you know the co-located keepers have an off-box backup.

- The **divergent same-name variants** (the SEAGATE vs SILVER/BLUE splits) — whether those are expected, or whether you want to investigate *why* two backup generations got the same name?
- The **keeper-placement logic** — e.g. always prefer `bhyslop-vault/` as one of the two, or some other rule for which copies survive?
- The **bobbie/ second NAS** — how it factors into "2 copies" (off-box copies as redundancy), and whether to verify its coverage first?
- **Execution mechanics** — the quarantine-vs-direct-delete tradeoff, or how `rm` interacts with the 98%-full volume?
- Something else entirely?

What's on your mind?

message.content: I don't precisely remember where we were.  I am quite ready to delete _redundant_ copies, and quite ready to have a single consolidated place for all 'singleton' copies.  Shall we do this before we then assess whether we have one unique version of everything from 'bobbie'?  the problem here is that I will have to release all of bobbie soon, and I want a reason to believe I have good copies.

content.text:
Let me re-orient you, since this has spanned a couple of days.

## Where we are

- The files live in two places: **local btrfs** (`fromdan/`, `bhyslop-vault/` — 126 archives, 8.52 TB) and the **second NAS `bobbie/`** mounted over SMB (148 archives, 9.8 TB).
- I've computed a **full md5 of every local file** (verified, no errors, bobbie never touched).
- Result: **27 distinct contents** locally, each with 2–8 redundant copies. **72 copies (4.72 TB) are exact-md5 duplicates** — safe to delete; the manifest above lists every one against its surviving twins.
- Along the way, full hashing caught real landmines: several `.broken`/`.diffOnBbdr`/`.REDO` files, and four same-name files that **split by source drive** (SEAGATE vs SILVER/BLUE), are *genuinely different content* — not duplicates. They're all preserved.

## Your goal reframed

You want, before releasing bobbie: **a consolidated local set you can trust contains a good copy of everything that's on bobbie.** That reorders things cleanly:

**Yes — do the local dedup + consolidation first. It's safe and independent of bobbie.** Deleting local exact-duplicates and gathering the survivors into one folder cannot reduce your bobbie coverage, because that's a separate comparison we run afterward.

But here's the critical part for the bobbie-release confidence: **I have *not* full-hashed bobbie** — only the unreliable head/tail fingerprint (the one that got fooled by `.broken`). To honestly claim "local has a good copy of everything on bobbie," I must **full-hash all of bobbie** (9.8 TB over SMB, ~25 h) and diff its md5 set against local. Any content that exists **only on bobbie** gets copied local *before* bobbie is released. That's the step that earns your "reason to believe."

So the order is:
1. **Delete the 72 redundant local copies** (4.72 TB; volume 98% → ~44%).
2. **Consolidate** the surviving unique archives into one place (I'd suggest `bhyslop-vault/` as the canonical home).
3. **Full-hash bobbie**, diff md5s, pull anything bobbie-only into the consolidated set.
4. **Release bobbie** with confidence.

One decision drives steps 1–2:

message.content: A key constraint: I do _not_ have SSH access to bobbie, so I think full hash is prohibitive.  Does SMB offer any help?  What else is 'cheap-ish'?

content.text:
Good constraint to surface — and it changes the math in your favor, not against.

**Does SMB help with hashing? No.** Without SSH (or an agent) on bobbie, there's no way to make bobbie's own CPU hash its files — SMB has no "hash this file" operation. Everything must be read *across* the wire and hashed here, which is the slow 9.8 TB/~25 h path. So full-hash is out.

**But SMB gives metadata for free, and you don't need to read whole files to catch your actual failure mode.** Your insight is the key: a USB-drive copy that goes wrong diverges *somewhere in the body* — and it's usually a substantial region, not one flipped bit. So:

**What's cheap-ish, in order of increasing cost:**
1. **Size** — free, already have all 277. Different size = definitely different. Necessary condition.
2. **Timestamps (mtime)** — one cheap `stat` per file over SMB. Same size+mtime ≈ same copy event.
3. **Head+tail fingerprint** — *already computed for bobbie* (those reads happened over SMB on day one). This is what got fooled by `.broken`, but it's still a useful filter.
4. **Strided sampling** — the real win: read, say, 64 evenly-spaced 1 MB windows *through the whole file* and hash them. For a 191 GB file that's 64 MB read — **~0.03% of a full hash** — yet it samples the middle, exactly where USB-copy divergence hides. All 148 bobbie files ≈ ~9 GB over SMB ≈ a few minutes, not 25 hours.

And you already predicted the punchline: most files will clear on **size + head/tail alone** (data I already have), leaving a short list that needs the strided check. Let me prove that right now — this costs nothing, it's all from existing data:

**Every single bobbie file already has a same-size, same-ends twin on the local volume.** There is no content that exists *only* on bobbie by this measure — which is exactly what you suspected: the issue list is short. In fact, at this resolution, it's empty.

**The one remaining risk is precisely your USB failure mode:** same size + same ends but a divergent middle — the `.broken` trap. That's what the cheap **strided sample** closes: 64 evenly-spaced 1 MB windows through each file (64 MB read regardless of file size), sampling the body where USB-copy corruption hides. ~9 GB over SMB total, a few minutes.

Note one subtlety it must handle: some local files of the same size are *themselves* two different versions (the SEAGATE-vs-SILVER splits). So I'll sample each bobbie file and match it against **every** distinct local version of that size — telling us not just "covered/not" but *which* version each bobbie copy corresponds to (or if it's a third, unseen variant).

input.command:
cd /tmp; awk -F'\t' '$2 ~ /7z$/ {print $3}' /tmp/nas_inventory.tsv > /tmp/strided_list.txt; wc -l < /tmp/strided_list.txt
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'cat > /tmp/strided_list.txt' < /tmp/strided_list.txt
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'cat > /tmp/strided.py' <<'PY'
import os,sys,hashlib
W=1<<20; K=64; ROOT="/volume1/homes/bhyslop"
out=open("/tmp/strided.tsv","w")
os.chdir(ROOT)
n=0
for line in open("/tmp/strided_list.txt"):
    p=line.rstrip("\n")
    if not p: continue
    try:
        sz=os.path.getsize(p); span=max(sz-W,0); h=hashlib.md5()
        with open(p,"rb") as f:
            for i in range(K):
                off=span*i//(K-1) if K>1 else 0
                f.seek(off); h.update(f.read(W))
        out.write(f"{sz}\t{h.hexdigest()}\t{p}\n"); out.flush()
    except Exception as e:
        out.write(f"ERR\t{e}\t{p}\n"); out.flush()
    n+=1
    open("/tmp/strided.progress","w").write(f"{n} done\n")
open("/tmp/strided.done","w").write("done\n")
PY
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'rm -f /tmp/strided.done /tmp/strided.progress; nohup python3 /tmp/strided.py >/tmp/strided.log 2>&1 </dev/null & echo "launched pid $!"'

input.command:
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'cat /tmp/strided.tsv' > /tmp/strided.tsv
cd /tmp; python3 - <<'PY'
import collections
strided={}  # path -> (size, sfp)
for line in open('/tmp/strided.tsv'):
    s,fp,p=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t'); strided[p]=(int(s),fp)
fullmd5={}  # local path -> md5
for line in open('/tmp/fullhash_results.tsv'):
    m,s,p=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t'); fullmd5[p]=m
def remote(p): return p.startswith('./bobbie/')

# (1) self-consistency: local files with same full md5 -> same strided print?
bad=0; bymd5=collections.defaultdict(set)
for p,m in fullmd5.items():
    bymd5[m].add(strided[p][1])
for m,sfps in bymd5.items():
    if len(sfps)>1: bad+=1; print("INCONSISTENT strided within md5",m[:12],sfps)
print(f"(1) self-consistency: {len(bymd5)} local md5 clusters, {bad} with mismatched strided prints  -> {'OK' if bad==0 else 'PROBLEM'}")

# build local reference: (size, strided) -> set(full md5)  and (size,strided)->paths
loc_sig2md5=collections.defaultdict(set)
for p,m in fullmd5.items():
    sz,sfp=strided[p]; loc_sig2md5[(sz,sfp)].add(m)
loc_sigs=set(loc_sig2md5)

# (2) bobbie coverage by (size, strided)
covered=0; uncovered=[]
for p,(sz,sfp) in strided.items():
    if not remote(p): continue
    if (sz,sfp) in loc_sigs: covered+=1
    else: uncovered.append((sz,p))
nb=sum(1 for p in strided if remote(p))
print(f"\n(2) bobbie coverage by size+strided-sample ({nb} bobbie files):")
print(f"    COVERED (matches a local content): {covered}")
print(f"    UNCOVERED (no local match)        : {len(uncovered)}")
for sz,p in sorted(uncovered): print(f"      {sz/1e9:7.1f}GB  {p}")

# (3) does bobbie add any NEW variant for the divergent-name archives?
print("\n(3) divergent-name archives — does bobbie introduce a 3rd version?")
names=['20230207-old-latitude-group.float2-1.7z','20230207-old-latitude-group.float1-1.7z',
       '20230210-gauntlet-enclosure.1-selections.7z','20230207-horde-black-seagate-usb.2.7z']
for nm in names:
    locsfp=set(); bobsfp=set()
    for p,(sz,sfp) in strided.items():
        if p.split('/')[-1]==nm:
            (bobsfp if remote(p) else locsfp).add(sfp)
    new=bobsfp-locsfp
    print(f"    {nm}: local {len(locsfp)} variant(s), bobbie adds {len(new)} new -> {'none new' if not new else new}")
PY

aiTitle: Deduplicate large password-protected files on NAS

content.text:
Confirmed — and it's the crucial caveat: `float1-1` and `horde.2` each have **two different full-md5 versions that produced the *identical* strided signature** (`b28d1741`, `482a8826`). The 64-window sample fell entirely in the gaps around a localized USB-copy difference. So strided sampling got fooled the same way head/tail did with `.broken`.

**Honest verdict on the cheap methods:** strided is a fine *screen* — it confirmed there's no bobbie-only content and no gross divergence (148/148 bobbie files map to a local content). But it is **not** proof of byte-identity for your exact failure mode. Only the full md5 distinguishes those close variants.

## Your 7z idea — yes, trust it; it's actually the *right* tool

`7z t` with the password is **more trustworthy than any hash for the question you actually care about.** Here's the distinction that matters:

- **A hash match** only tells you two files are *identical* — they could be identically corrupt (both copied from the same bad source).
- **`7z t` tells you the archive is *internally intact*** — it decrypts and decompresses every member and verifies each one's stored **CRC32**. That's an integrity certificate of the data itself, independent of any other copy.

That black/white behavior you remember is real: it prints `Everything is Ok`, or it names the failure (`CRC Failed`, `Data Error`, `Unexpected end of archive`, `Wrong password`). No ambiguity.

**Where it's strong / where to be aware:**
- ✅ Catches truncation, bit-rot, incomplete USB copies, decryption failure — exactly your failure mode.
- ✅ **It adjudicates your divergent pairs definitively.** Run it on both the SEAGATE and SILVER versions of `horde.2`: the bad-USB-copy one fails CRC, the good one passes. That *resolves* "which is the real one" — something hashing can never do.
- ⚠️ It's a **full read** — same I/O cost as a full md5 (~2.5–3 h for one copy of each distinct archive), and it needs the password.
- ⚠️ CRC32 is 32-bit, so a corrupted file has a ~1-in-4-billion chance of passing by fluke. Irrelevant for random USB errors; it's only weak against a deliberate adversary, which you don't have.
- ⚠️ It certifies the archive is *self-consistent*, not that the original data was captured correctly in 2008/2023 — it can't know what *should* be inside.

## How this reframes everything

The bobbie-vs-local byte comparison was only ever a *proxy* for "do I have a good copy." **`7z t` answers that directly.** So the capstone is clean:

1. Run `7z t` on **one copy of each of the 27 distinct contents** (each keeper's twin is byte-identical, so one test certifies both). ~1.65 TB, ~3 h.
2. Every `PASS` = a certified-good local copy of that content. The 4 divergent pairs get *both* sides tested automatically (each side is its own md5 cluster), adjudicating them.
3. With strided already showing **no bobbie-only content**, a clean sweep of `7z t` passes is your "reason to believe" — **then release bobbie.**
4. *After* certification, do the local dedup/consolidate (keep 2), now knowing exactly which copies are gold.

This also retroactively settles whether the `.broken`/`.broken2`/`.diffOnBbdr` files are actually broken — `7z t` will tell you black/white.

I can build a detached `7z t` harness over the 27 representatives that records PASS/FAIL per archive. Two things I need from you:

content.text:
Great — and your instinct about speed is correct, which makes this even better.

**Does `7z t` check every byte? Yes — and here's why it's faster than md5 on this box.** `7z t` reads the entire archive from disk and decrypts + decompresses every member to verify each one's CRC32. So I/O-wise it touches all the bytes, exactly like a hash. The reason it's *wall-clock faster* than `md5sum` on your DS223j:

- `md5sum` is **single-threaded, scalar** — one of four cores, no acceleration, stuck at ~150–250 MB/s.
- `7z` is **multi-threaded** and the Cortex-A55 has **ARMv8 hardware AES**, so decryption is accelerated and decompression spreads across all four cores. It ends up disk-bound rather than CPU-bound.

So `7z t` does *more* verification work (decrypt + decompress + CRC vs. a plain hash) yet finishes sooner. You picked the method that is both **more authoritative and faster.** Hashing was always the wrong hammer for "is this archive good" — it only ever told us "identical," never "intact." `7z t` tells you intact, byte-coverage included.

## Your workflow → my job

You'll run `7z t` manually per archive (each with its own password); I keep a ledger of what's done and the verdict, never seeing any password. You just tell me the filename + result (`Everything is Ok` / the error), and I track it.

**One big effort-saver:** you do **not** need to test all 126 local copies. Byte-identical copies are equally valid — if one passes, every copy with the same md5 passes. So you only need **27 tests, one per distinct content.** I'll give you the exact representative path for each. Let me build that ledger.

input.command:
cd /tmp; python3 - <<'PY'
import collections
rows=[]  # (md5,size,path)
for line in open('/tmp/fullhash_results.tsv'):
    m,s,p=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t'); rows.append((m,int(s),p))
def bn(p): return p.split('/')[-1]
clusters=collections.defaultdict(list)
for m,s,p in rows: clusters[m].append((s,p))
# basenames per md5, and md5s per basename (to flag divergent)
md5s_by_base=collections.defaultdict(set)
for m,s,p in rows: md5s_by_base[bn(p)].add(m)
def rep(paths):  # prefer bhyslop-vault, else shortest path
    v=[p for s,p in paths if p.startswith('./bhyslop-vault/')]
    return sorted(v or [p for s,p in paths], key=len)[0]
lines=[]
lines.append("# 7z Integrity Certification Ledger")
lines.append("")
lines.append("Run `7z t <path>` (with that archive's password) on the **Representative** below.")
lines.append("One pass certifies all byte-identical copies. Report filename + verdict; I update Status.")
lines.append("")
lines.append("| # | Status | Size | Archive | md5 | Copies | Representative to test |")
lines.append("|---|--------|------|---------|-----|--------|------------------------|")
items=[]
for m,paths in clusters.items():
    s=paths[0][0]; b=bn(paths[0][1])
    items.append((b,m,s,len(paths),rep(paths)))
for i,(b,m,s,n,r) in enumerate(sorted(items,key=lambda x:(x[0],x[1])),1):
    div=" ⚠️divergent" if len(md5s_by_base[b])>1 else ""
    lines.append(f"| {i} | ⬜ PENDING | {s/1e9:.1f}GB | {b}{div} | `{m[:10]}` | {n} | `{r}` |")
txt="\n".join(lines)+"\n"
open('/tmp/7z-cert-ledger.md','w').write(txt)
print(txt)
PY
# durable copy on the NAS home (local btrfs, survives /tmp wipes)
ssh bhyslop-nas2 'cat > ~/7z-cert-ledger.md' < /tmp/7z-cert-ledger.md && echo "ledger saved to NAS ~/7z-cert-ledger.md"

Then just tell me the row # (or filename) and the verdict — `Everything is Ok`, or the error text. No password ever comes to me. I'll flip ⬜ PENDING → ✅ PASS / ❌ FAIL and keep the running tally.

**These rows will resolve the open mysteries — worth prioritizing:**

The four **⚠️ divergent** archives are head-to-head version contests. `7z t` declares the winner (the good copy); a loser that fails CRC is your bad USB copy, safe to drop:

| Archive | Version A | Version B |
|---|---|---|
| old-latitude **float1-1** (13.1 GB) | row **1** (`5a7efa8e`, via the misnamed `float1-xxx`) | row **15** (`197c4853`, SEAGATE) |
| old-latitude **float2-1** (12.3 GB) | row **16** (`02ab1f7b`, SEAGATE) | row **17** (`f072052d`, vault/BLUE) |
| **gauntlet** selections (73.8 GB) | row **20** (`028ffba5`, SILVER) | row **21** (`955457d7`, vault/SEAGATE) |
| **horde-...usb.2** (191.4 GB) | row **13** (`67976498`, SILVER) | row **14** (`d041e999`, SEAGATE) |

And the **"broken"-named ones finally get adjudicated** — rows 4, 11, 19, 22, 25, 26 (`.broken1`, `.diffOnBbdr`, `.REDO`, `.broken`, `.broken2`). `7z t` tells you black/white whether those labels were accurate or just cautious naming.

**Sequencing reminder:** once these come back clean, combined with the strided result (no content lives only on bobbie), you'll have your certified-good local set — *then* release bobbie, *then* I run the keep-2 dedup/consolidation on the proven-good copies.

No rush and no particular order — these are big files and real passwords. Report them as you go (one at a time is fine), and I'll keep the board current. Want me to start a fresh tracking window now, or are you set to begin?

message.content: Did we do the 2 copy reduction and corralling?  Should we do that first?

content.text:
No — **nothing has been deleted or moved.** Everything so far has been read-only (hashing, fingerprinting, strided sampling, ledger). The volume is still 98% full with all 126 local copies present.

**Should we do the dedup + corral first? Yes — and it's safe to, here's why:**

The dedup only collapses copies that are **byte-identical by full md5** (proven, not assumed), keeping 2 of each distinct content — and crucially it keeps **both** versions of every divergent pair. So:

- Deleting a redundant copy can't cost you a good copy: its surviving twin is *bit-for-bit the same file*. Whatever `7z t` later says about that content applies equally to all its copies.
- No premature commitment on the divergent contests: both versions stay until `7z t` declares a loser (a separate, later decision).

And the payoff is real: frees **~4.72 TB (98% → ~44%)**, and a tidy consolidated layout makes the 27 manual `7z t` tests far easier to navigate.

**One bookkeeping consequence:** corralling moves some files, so the ledger's representative paths would change. I'll **regenerate the ledger against the final consolidated paths** right after — so when you sit down to test, you're testing files in their permanent home.

So the revised order is: **dedup + corral now → regenerate ledger → you run the 27 `7z t` tests on the clean set → release bobbie.**

Two things to lock before I touch anything (deletion is the one irreversible step):

aiTitle: Deduplicate large password-protected files on NAS

content.text:
Locked in: **two consolidated folders** (`bhyslop-vault/` canonical + `bhyslop-vault-mirror/`), **quarantine-then-delete**. Since all of this is on one btrfs volume, every move is an instant rename — fully reversible until the final purge of `_TO_DELETE/`.

There's one naming snag I have to resolve and want your eyes on: the **4 divergent archives have two versions sharing one filename** — they'd collide in a flat folder. So I'll disambiguate those 8 files with a short md5 suffix (and normalize the `float1-xxx` typo back to its real name). Everything else keeps its original name. Let me generate the full move plan for review before touching anything.

input.command:
cd /tmp; python3 - <<'PY'
import collections,os
rows=[]
for line in open('/tmp/fullhash_results.tsv'):
    m,s,p=line.rstrip('\n').split('\t'); rows.append((m,int(s),p))
def bn(p): return p.split('/')[-1]
def invault(p): return p.startswith('./bhyslop-vault/')
clusters=collections.defaultdict(list)
for m,s,p in rows: clusters[m].append(p)
# which basenames are shared by >1 md5 (divergent -> need md5 suffix)
md5s_by_base=collections.defaultdict(set)
for m,s,p in rows: md5s_by_base[bn(p)].add(m)
def canon_name(m, sample_paths):
    bases=collections.Counter(bn(p) for p in sample_paths)
    # normalize the float1-xxx typo to the real name if present in cluster
    names=set(bases)
    base=bases.most_common(1)[0][0]
    if 'float1-xxx' in base:
        # use the proper float1-1 name shared by siblings
        alt=[b for b in names if 'float1-1' in b]
        if alt: base=alt[0]
    divergent = len(md5s_by_base[bn(sample_paths[0])])>1 or any(len(md5s_by_base[b])>1 for b in names)
    if divergent:
        stem,ext=base.rsplit('.',1)
        return f"{stem}.{m[:8]}.{ext}", True
    return base, False

canon_moves=[]; mirror_moves=[]; quarantine=[]; renames=[]
for m,paths in clusters.items():
    name,isdiv = canon_name(m,paths)
    # canonical source: prefer an existing vault copy
    vault_copies=[p for p in paths if invault(p)]
    nonvault=[p for p in paths if not invault(p)]
    if vault_copies:
        csrc=vault_copies[0]; rest=[p for p in paths if p!=csrc]
    else:
        csrc=nonvault[0]; rest=[p for p in paths if p!=csrc]
    cdst=f"./bhyslop-vault/{name}"
    if csrc!=cdst: canon_moves.append((csrc,cdst))
    if os.path.basename(csrc)!=name: renames.append((csrc,name))
    # mirror source: prefer a non-vault remaining copy
    msrc=([p for p in rest if not invault(p)] or rest)[0]
    rest=[p for p in rest if p!=msrc]
    mdst=f"./bhyslop-vault-mirror/{name}"
    mirror_moves.append((msrc,mdst))
    if os.path.basename(msrc)!=name and (msrc,name) not in renames: pass
    # rest -> quarantine preserving path
    for p in rest: quarantine.append((p, "./_TO_DELETE/"+p[2:]))

print(f"PLAN: 27 contents -> vault(27) + mirror(27) = 54 kept; quarantine {len(quarantine)} (delete after eyeball)\n")
print("=== RENAMES (divergent disambiguation + typo fix) — the only name changes ===")
for src,name in sorted(renames):
    print(f"  {bn(src):52} ->  {name}")
print(f"\n=== bhyslop-vault/ canonical copies that must MOVE in (not already there): {len(canon_moves)} ===")
for s,d in sorted(canon_moves,key=lambda x:x[1]):
    print(f"  {d[16:]:60} <= {s}")
print(f"\nquarantine sample (first 6 of {len(quarantine)}):")
for s,d in quarantine[:6]: print(f"  {s}")
# persist plan
import json
json.dump({'canon':canon_moves,'mirror':mirror_moves,'quarantine':quarantine}, open('/tmp/corral_plan.json','w'))
print("\nplan saved -> /tmp/corral_plan.json")
PY

import collections,os
rows=[]

def bn(p): return p.split('/')[-1]
clusters=collections.defaultdict(list)
for m,s,p in rows: clusters[m].append(p)
md5s_by_base=collections.defaultdict(set)
for m,s,p in rows: md5s_by_base[bn(p)].add(m)
def canon_name(m,paths):
    bases=collections.Counter(bn(p) for p in paths); names=set(bases); base=bases.most_common(1)[0][0]

        alt=[b for b in names if 'float1-1' in b]; base=alt[0] if alt else base
    divergent = any(len(md5s_by_base[b])>1 for b in names)
    if divergent:
        stem,ext=base.rsplit('.',1); return f"{stem}.{m[:8]}.{ext}",True
    return base,False

content.text:
**Quarantine is verified safe to purge: 0 orphans.** All 72 quarantined files are byte-identical (by full md5) to a retained `vault + mirror` pair. Nothing unique is in `_TO_DELETE/`.

## Current state

| | |
|---|---|
| `~/bhyslop-vault/` | 27 canonical archives (clean names) |
| `~/bhyslop-vault-mirror/` | 27 identical copies (your 2nd copy) |
| `~/_TO_DELETE/` | 72 redundant copies, 4.8 TB — all proven duplicates |
| Ledger | rebuilt at `~/7z-cert-ledger.md`, 27 rows pointing at vault |
| Disk | still 98% — frees to **~44%** the moment we purge |

A useful note on the safety question: holding the quarantine does **not** add integrity protection — those copies are bit-for-bit identical to the vault pair, so if a vault copy were bad, the quarantined one would be identically bad. Its only value is as a buffer against a mistake in my corral, and since btrfs renames are metadata-only (they can't alter file data), there's no real risk there either. So purging now is rational — but it's your call, and it's the one irreversible step.

Two independent things you can now do, in any order:

1. **Purge `_TO_DELETE/`** → frees 4.7 TB (98% → ~44%). Want me to show the full 72-path list first, or are you set to purge?
2. **Begin `7z t` certification** — the vault files are in their permanent home, so you can start testing the 27 representatives whenever (this is read-only and needs no free space). Report verdicts by row # and I'll keep the ledger current.

How do you want to proceed?

content: Goal: dedup the NAS archives and certify good copies before releasing the bobbie NAS. Done: 27 unique archives consolidated into vault plus mirror, 72 duplicates quarantined (4.8 TB). Next: tell me to purge _TO_DELETE, or start the 27 7z password tests. (disable recaps in /config)

message.content: I am going to save this chat for later, when I am at home and feel safe viewing my password keeper.  Those are sensitive.  We won't purge yet.  Hmm, I have another idea: if I resume this chat in tmux, can we copy all the 'keepers' to the filesystem of cerebro?  I think it has plenty of space, and the work can happen while I detach, right?

content.text:
Yes to all of that — and it's a great idea. Two layers keep it alive while you're detached:

- **tmux** keeps the Claude Code session itself alive across detach/reconnect.
- **The transfer itself runs detached** (`nohup`/background), exactly like the hashing runs we've been doing — so it survives even if tmux, SSH, or your laptop drops. You truly can walk away.

And copying the keepers to cerebro is more than convenient — it gives you a **third copy on a separate machine**, which is precisely the "good copies before bobbie goes away" reassurance you wanted.

Since this needs no passwords (the archives copy as opaque encrypted blobs — bobbie-style, no keeper involved), let me do a quick **read-only pre-flight now** so the resume is turnkey. Checking how the NAS reaches cerebro, space, and tooling:

input.command:
echo "=== can THIS station reach cerebro? ==="; ssh -o ConnectTimeout=8 -o BatchMode=yes cerebro 'hostname; echo "space:"; df -hT . 2>/dev/null | tail -1; echo "tools:"; for t in rsync md5sum xxhsum b3sum; do printf "%s=" $t; command -v $t >/dev/null && echo yes || echo no; done; echo "nproc: $(nproc)"' 2>&1 | head -20
echo
echo "=== can the NAS reach cerebro directly (for NAS->cerebro rsync)? ==="
ssh -o ConnectTimeout=8 bhyslop-nas2 'ssh -o ConnectTimeout=8 -o BatchMode=yes cerebro "echo NAS_TO_CEREBRO_OK; hostname" 2>&1 | head -3; echo "rsync on NAS: $(command -v rsync || echo MISSING)"'

Useful pre-flight results — neither machine can talk to the other *yet*, but the pieces are there:

- **This station → cerebro:** reachable, but `Host key verification failed` — cerebro isn't in this station's `known_hosts` (just needs a one-time key accept).
- **NAS → cerebro:** `Could not resolve hostname cerebro` — the NAS has no name/route to cerebro, and no key. But **rsync is installed on the NAS**, which is what matters.

So the data path NAS↔cerebro isn't wired up. Let me see how *this* station resolves cerebro, so I know its real address for the resume:

input.command:
echo "=== cerebro in ssh config ==="; awk 'BEGIN{IGNORECASE=1} /^Host /{h=$0} /cerebro/{print FILENAME": "$0}' ~/.ssh/config 2>/dev/null; grep -iA6 "Host.*cerebro" ~/.ssh/config 2>/dev/null
echo "=== how cerebro resolves ==="; getent hosts cerebro 2>/dev/null || echo "(not in DNS/hosts on station)"
echo "=== NAS address (for cerebro->NAS pull) ==="; getent hosts bhyslop-nas2 2>/dev/null; ssh -G bhyslop-nas2 2>/dev/null | awk '/^hostname /{print "nas hostname: "$2}/^user /{print "nas user: "$2}'
echo "=== is there a tailnet? (bobbie was 192.168.3.x) ==="; ssh bhyslop-nas2 'ip -4 addr 2>/dev/null | grep -oE "inet [0-9.]+" | head; echo "tailscale: $(command -v tailscale || echo none)"'
input.description: Discover cerebro/NAS addressing and network topology
